text.title: competitive-intelligence Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for competitive-intelligence. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every loss-reason row requires at least one verbatim buyer-side quote with speaker role, company size, and segment. Rep notes alone are insufficient — set confidence to 2 or below and flag for interview.
rule.rationale: Win/loss self-report bias: sales reps over-attribute losses to "price" when buyers actually leave for product fit, integration risk, or relationship gaps. Buyer-side quotes override rep attribution.

rule.statement: Maintain maximum 8 loss reasons in the taxonomy. Never expand silently — taxonomy additions require a documented decision. Deals that do not fit get "other" and go to human triage.
rule.rationale: Taxonomy drift breaks cross-period trend lines. A reason added in Q3 cannot be compared to Q1 data unless it is backfilled consistently.

rule.statement: Treat "no_decision" as its own outcome, not as a loss. Report stalled-pipeline rate separately from competitive loss rate.
rule.rationale: Mixing no-decision with competitive losses inflates the win-rate denominator and makes pipeline health look worse than it is. Leadership uses the wrong metric to judge sales effectiveness.

rule.statement: Run buyer-side win/loss interviews on at least 30% of lost deals above $50K ACV. Use Clozd or an internal interviewer who never sat on the deal — reps cannot interview their own losses.
rule.rationale: Rep-interviewed losses produce systematically biased data defending the rep's narrative. Independent interviewers surface the actual buyer motivations that drove the decision.

rule.statement: Stratify all win/loss analysis by segment and deal size. "We lose 60% of deals" is meaningless; "we lose 80% of mid-market deals against Vendor X when integration matters" is actionable.
rule.rationale: Blended win rates hide segment-specific patterns that require different responses. A 60% blended rate could mean 90% win in enterprise and 30% win in SMB — opposite strategic implications.

rule.statement: Triangulate competitor pricing three ways: posted list price, G2 reviewer-disclosed spend, and ex-customer or buyer interview. Publish the median and the spread — not a single number.
rule.rationale: B2B SaaS discounting averages 15-35% off list price. Published list price is aspirational; realized ACV from buyer disclosures is the actual competitive benchmark. Single-source pricing intel is misleading.

rule.statement: For marketplaces, always compute all-in take rate: commission + payment processing + seller subscription + ad platform fees. Never report headline commission alone.
rule.rationale: Headline commission (e.g., Etsy 6.5%) understates the real take by 2x or more when seller subscriptions, payment fees, and ad revenue are included. All-in rate is what sellers actually experience.

rule.statement: Require at least 3 independent fetches of a competitor pricing page over 14 days before declaring a pricing change. Single-fetch divergence is likely AB testing, not a price change.
rule.rationale: Competitors run pricing AB tests continuously; a daily fetch sees noise. Premature "competitor dropped prices" signals based on one fetch trigger incorrect strategic responses.

rule.statement: Pin currency, billing period, unit (per-seat / per-org / per-usage), and contract length in the pricing schema. "$10/month" without these fields cannot be compared across competitors.
rule.rationale: Currency and term ambiguity is the number-one cause of pricing benchmark errors. A per-seat monthly price cannot be added to a per-org annual price without normalization.

rule.statement: Positioning extracted from buyer reviews outweighs positioning declared by competitor marketing copy. Hero copy reflects aspiration; G2 reviews reflect what buyers actually experience. The gap is your wedge.
rule.rationale: Competitors write hero copy for the positioning they want; buyers describe the product they got. The gap between declared positioning and buyer-described positioning is where an entrant can credibly differentiate.

rule.statement: Use k-means with manual seed labels per quarter; reject auto-clustering output. Re-run with the same seed labels each quarter to maintain trend comparability.
rule.rationale: Auto-clustering by LLMs merges adjacent categories that are actually distinct buyer segments with different purchase criteria. Manual seed labels preserve the signal that category definitions are shifting.

rule.statement: Re-baseline positioning analysis every quarter. Publish dated artifacts (competitive-analysis-YYYY-MM-DD.md). A stale market map is worse than no map — it guides decisions toward the wrong target.
rule.rationale: Pricing, packaging, and positioning shift continuously. Sales enablement teams trust dated artifacts because they know what version they are working from.
